coredata.dc:title: Sonochemical green reduction to prepare Ag nanoparticles decorated graphene sheets for catalytic performance and antibacterial application
coredata.dc:creator: Ganguly, Sayan
Das, Poushali
Bose, Madhuparna
Das, Tushar Kanti
Mondal, Subhadip
Das, Amit Kumar
Das, Narayan C.
coredata.dc:description: The emerging popularity and wide acceptance of green chemistry and environmentally benign/ecofriendly approaches have comprehensively considered for catalyst synthesis methods. Natural resource derived carbogenic quantum dots has been used in assistance with ultrasonic shock wave to graphene oxide (GO) aqueous dispersion in order to prepare reduced graphene oxide decorated with silver nanoparticles following the ‘top-down’ method. The total reduction process is done without using any toxic external reducing agents and any surfactants or stabilizers, thus it can be accepted as green method. Sonochemical destratification of the GO layers provides green attributes due to scalable, non-hazardous and relatively fast reduction to enhance surface area of the GO. Arresting the silver nanoparticles onto basal planes of graphene oxide can act as an efficient solid state support catalyst for fast reduction of toxic nitro aryls. Besides this work also reports bactericidal feature exhibited by the catalyst. Thus a dual functioning nanomaterial has been successfully developed which can be a suitable alternative for reductive forthcoming specialty/multifunctional membrane and other high-end medicinal or industrial applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green synthesis
Carbogenic quantum dots
Catalyst
Antibacterial

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Proposed mechanistic illustration for RGO-Ag synthesis
Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and Zeta potential analysis
Reduction kinetics of 4-NP by RGO-Ag nanocomposite
Turn of frequency (TOF) and reusability of the catalyst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Overview of the preparation and catalytic activity towards 4-NP reduction.
Plausible mechanism for reduction of GO by g-CDs.

Schematic illustration of the ultrasonication process based on the acoustic cavitation. High intensity of alternating expansive and compressive cycles can be generated by sonotrode of an ultrasonicator. Such phenomenon causes the consecutive steps of generation, growth, and collapse of bubbles, which successively outputs acute shockwaves that elevate inter-particle collisions. Inter-particle collision results the exfoliation/destratification of the layered platelets.
(a) XRD plot of GO and RGO-Ag nanocomposite (b) UV–vis spectra of GO and prepared RGO-Ag (insets of the graphs the digital images of GO and RGO-Ag has been displayed).
HRTEM images of (a) GO nano-sheets (b) AgNPs decorated over RGO sheets (inset: SAED pattern of the RGO-Ag nanocomposite) and (c) magnified image of one AgNP having lattice fringe (0.242nm).
FESEM images of (a) GO sheets (b) RGO-Ag and (c) EDX of RGO-Ag (inset: Ag mapping over the highlighted area of (b)).
XPS survey scan of (a) RGO-Ag composite (b) high resolution Ag 3d core-level spectra and (c) deconvoluted XPS spectra for C1s.

(a) Adsorption spectra of pure 4-NP (b) Absorption spectra of 4-NP in presence of NaBH4 at 1min to 8min with respectively. Conditions: [4-NP]=1.0×10−4 M; [RGO-Ag]=1.2gL−1; [NaBH4]=0.1M, (b) Effect of NaBH4 concentration on catalytic reduction of 4-NP in the presence of RGO-Ag nanocomposite with conditions: [4-NP]=1.0×10−4 M and [RGO-Ag]=1.2gL−1 (c) Plot of ln(Ct/C0) versus time (min) for the reduction of 4-NP with NaBH4 in the presence of RGO-Ag nanoparticles (d) Time-dependent absorbance plot at 400nm for the reduction of 4- NP with NaBH4 in the presence of RGO-Ag nanoparticles.
Digital photograph of agar plate with inhibition zone of RGO-Ag nanocomposite against E. coli.
Schematic illustration of ‘attack and kill’ mechanism performed by RGO-Ag nanocomposite.
SEM images of E. coli cells (a) without and (b) with the treatment of 1g/L RGO-Ag nanocomposite.
Diagrammatic illustration of the preparation of g-CDs.
Pseudo first-order rate constants (k) and turnover frequencies (TOF) for the reduction of 4-NP with NaBH4 in the presence of RGO-Ag nanocomposite.
Comparative studies of the catalytic activities of various reported catalysts and our work.

head.ce:title: Sonochemical green reduction to prepare Ag nanoparticles decorated graphene sheets for catalytic performance and antibacterial application

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Rubber Technology Centre, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur, 721302, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Nanoscience and Technology, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur, 721302, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biotechnology, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur, 721302, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Rubber Technology Centre, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur, 721302, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The emerging popularity and wide acceptance of green chemistry and environmentally benign/ecofriendly approaches have comprehensively considered for catalyst synthesis methods. Natural resource derived carbogenic quantum dots has been used in assistance with ultrasonic shock wave to graphene oxide (GO) aqueous dispersion in order to prepare reduced graphene oxide decorated with silver nanoparticles following the ‘top-down’ method. The total reduction process is done without using any toxic external reducing agents and any surfactants or stabilizers, thus it can be accepted as green method. Sonochemical destratification of the GO layers provides green attributes due to scalable, non-hazardous and relatively fast reduction to enhance surface area of the GO. Arresting the silver nanoparticles onto basal planes of graphene oxide can act as an efficient solid state support catalyst for fast reduction of toxic nitro aryls. Besides this work also reports bactericidal feature exhibited by the catalyst. Thus a dual functioning nanomaterial has been successfully developed which can be a suitable alternative for reductive forthcoming specialty/multifunctional membrane and other high-end medicinal or industrial applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Two dimensional graphene nanosheets, comprising sp2 hybridised catenated carbons have met significant attention due to its unique electronic, mechanical and thermal properties since its birth in the year of 2004 [1]. This 2D architectural marvel with ornamentation by metal nanoparticles like Au, Ag, Pt, Pd, Cu and TiO2 has been nurtured in recent years after [2,3]. When these metallic nanoparticles get attached to the graphene basal plane, the outcome property leads to some surprising electronic, optical, sensor and catalytic property. These attributes are the motivational breakthrough of several unique applications like chemosensor, photocatalysis, optoelectronics and some biological high end uses. From the synthesis and application point of view nano-metal based graphene composites have been practiced for development of novel physico-chemical catalysts [4,5].

Some synthetic methods to prepare nanosilver decorated graphene have already been reported in literatures. A very common reducing agent hydrazine was used by Pasricha et al. for preparing Ag-reduced graphene oxide nanocomposites [6]. Tien and his coworkers reported another graphene decorated with silver nanoparticles by two-step reduction by glycol and sodium borohydride [7]. For biomaterial synthesis, Shen et al. developed chemically converted graphene-Ag composites [8]. All these mentioned processes required complicated methods, sophisticated experimental condition, rigorous reaction conditions, post treatment, time consuming factor and toxic reducing materials. On the point of environmental issues, these methods and mode of operations already have been limited in several domains. Therefore, facile, non-toxic green process is necessary to synthesize silver decorated reduced graphene oxide by adopting the controllable reactions parameters, shorter time and sonochemistry driven synthesis [9,10]. Sonochemical synthesis is an eco-friendly approach to generate nanoparticles by top-down method obeying the concept of de-aggregation [11–13]. Ultrasound energy generates acoustic cavitation which results formation, nucleation/growth and collapsing of bubble/vesicles in liquid environment consecutively [14,15]. Collapsing of bubbles evolves intense thermal energy and pressure within a very short period of time. According to literature it was documented that sonication method evolves 5000K temperature, 20MPa pressure and high cooling rate of 1010 K/s [16,17]. At the time of treatment via sonication, acoustic cavitation supplies the effective environment for reaction without using any rigorous reducing agent. Hence, it can be said that sonochemical method substantiates the principles of green chemistry [18–20].

Our attempt lies on the basis of green reduction by carbon dots (C-dots) derived from some bio-macromolecules driven by ultrasonic waves. There are very few reports available on this preparation of biogenically derived carbon dots as reducing agents. Now it is an obvious question why these carbon dots have been taken as a reducing agent instead of other conventional reducing agents. On account of this, C-dots are very much acceptable due to their significant electron transfer behaviours, photostability and other optoelectronic features [21]. The electron transfer behaviour of C-dots can make it a successful agent for catalytic/reduction applications [22]. Nonetheless, C-dots have sufficient biocompatibility and less-toxicity with respect to other semiconductor quantum dots [23]. Very few reports have been published on the biogenically derived C-dots like dehydration of carbohydrates by strong acids, oxidation of capsicum extract [24,25], lemon extract [26], carbonized gram shell [27], utilizing orange peel [28], milk [29], apple extract [30], soya milk [31] etc. Inspiring from the green methods for reduction, our choice went with a naturally obtained biopolymer; gelatin. Gelatin is a widely known biopolymer for its food grade biocompatibility, gelling tendency, rheology modifying characters and stabilization.

In present days metal nanoparticles (MNPs) decorated graphene nano-composites become an efficient reusable supported catalysts system for rapid waste water treatment [32]. There are several researchers already proposed dye degradation catalysts with metal nanoparticles supported on eggshell, Fe3O4, titania, zeolite, graphene oxide and metals oxides [33–35]. These supports have been taken to avoid the self-aggregation, low stability and complicated recovery of metal nanoparticles. Herein, a green method has been developed to prepare such metal nanoparticles decorated reduce graphene oxide. Our prepared material has extraordinary catalytic activity towards dye degradation. Sonochemistry induced bio-reduction method by biopolymer derived carbogenic dots is unique which has not been reported as of our knowledge.

Herein, our main aim is to develop a sustainable and greener approach for RGO-Ag nanocomposite with the aid of bio-based environment friendly C-dots within a short time span by ultrasonic wave. To the best of our knowledge, no previous studies reported in the literature towards the fabrication of RGO-Ag nanocomposite using carbon dots as green reducing agent. Water dispersible C-dots with high quantum yield (31.6%) and superior luminescence properties were obtained from gelatin by simple thermal carbonization without using any strong acid or surface passivating agent. These C-dots assisted green reduction of RGO-Ag nanocomposite requires an ultra-low concentration of C-dots as reducing agent. Water soluble bio-polymer gelatin mainly derived from animal bones and skin which is as an inexpensive source. By using a very little concentration of C-dots from a low-cost source permit large-scale production of RGO-Ag nanocomposite without using any rigorous chemicals and tedious extraction or purification methods. The presence of carboxylic (–COOH), hydroxyl (–OH) and amine (–NH2) moieties at the surface of the C-dots acts as nucleation centers for the growth of silver nanoparticles. Moreover, the inherent reducing property of C-dots enables them for reduction of silver salts to the corresponding silver nanoparticles. Realizing the potential of the gelatin derived C-dots as stabilizing and reducing agents towards the development of nanocomposite will certainly lead to eco-friendly pavement for nano catalysis. The nanocomposite exhibits dual performances like engrossing catalytic activity and bactericidal effect towards gram-negative bacteria E. coli. A significant advantage in the performance aspect of the RGO-Ag nanocomposite is its reusability up to 5 times without deactivation of the catalyst and very rapid (7min) reduction compare to other contemporary works [36–40]. The ease of synthesis and excellent performance of the RGO-Ag nanocomposite make these inexpensive nanocomposite attractive not for future nanocatalyst but also for large-scale applications in high-performance catalysis and bactericidal agents. This research can be imposing promising effectiveness and further applicability for fabrication of antibacterial catalytic membranes.

Graphite powder, sufphuric acid (H2SO4, 98%), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%), potassium permanganate (KMnO4), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), quinine sulfate (C20H24N2O2·0.5H2O4S·H2O), sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and silver nitrate (AgNO3) were procured from Sigma Aldrich. Gelatin and phosphate-bufferedsaline (PBS) were obtained from Sigma and used as received. Escherichia coli (DH5α) used in this experiment were obtained from Department of Biotechnology, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur, India. All other chemicals used were of reagent grade.

Graphite oxide was prepared as per the modified Hummer’s method from graphite flakes as the precursor material [41]. Briefly, 3g graphite flakes were dispersed in 65mL 98% sulphuric acid in an ice bath. After that 9g KMnO4 was added slowly for 45min and kept for stirring for 3h. Then 90mL deionized water was added slowly into the reaction mixture, resulting a dark brown solution which was terminated after addition of more water (120mL) and 25mL of 30% H2O2. After stirring the whole dispersion for 1h, the product was accumulated by centrifugation at 8000rpm for 10min. The collected product was dried under vacuum overnight at 60°C.

Gelatin derived C-dots (g-CDs) were prepared by carbonization of pure gelatin powder. 400mg of gelatin powder was dissolved in deionized water (10mL) to prepare a clear solution. Then the homogeneous clear solution was poured in an alumina crucible followed by heating in a muffle furnace at 220°C for 30min. After 30min the carbonized solid mass was crushed into fine powder and leached with hot water. The supernatant was collected after centrifugation the dispersion at 12,000rpm for 45min. The entire process has been illustrated in scheme 1 .

Ag-RGO nanocomposite were prepared by in situ sonochemical method by gelatin derived C-dots. The schematic overview of the entire preparation and catalytic activity were represented in Fig. 1 . 200mg of GO was dispersed in 75mL deionized water by sonication for 30min to form a stable GO dispersion. After that an aqueous solution of AgNO3 (0.002mol) was slowly added to the prepared GO dispersion and stirred for 30min. Gelatin derived carbon dots (g –CDs) with concentration of 7mg/mL was then added into the mixture and sonicated (sonics VCX 750, 750W, 20kHz) for 1h. During the whole process the pH of the medium was around 7.7–7.9. The greyish black mass was separated by centrifugation at 5000rpm for 15min and washed with deionized water-ethanol mixture (3:1 v/v). The final product was dried under vacuum at 60°C for overnight.

Catalytic activity of ultrasound assisted bio-reduced RGO-Ag nanocomposite was assessed after monitoring the 4-NP reduction in well-stoppered quartz cell in presence of aqueous borohydride. During the catalytic activity study, the catalyst (RGO-Ag) dose was altered in the range of 0.5–2g/L, keeping the NaBH4 concentration at 0.1M. In the quartz cuvette, 2.5mL of 0.1M NaBH4, 25μL 4-NP aliquote from stock solution (0.01M) was placed. At this level, the 4-NP was turned to nitrophenolate anion. Then the catalyst was added followed by spectroscopic monitoring of the reaction mixture at 400nm at different time intervals. Similarly, as of before, the study was also performed at several initial concentration of 4-NP in the range of 0.6–1.4×10−4 M keeping the catalyst dose fixed at 1.5g/L and borohydride concentration at 0.1M. To investigate the effect of borohydride concentration, the NaBH4 concentration was also varied from 0.03 to 0.1M keeping the catalyst dose and 4-NP fixed at 1.5g/L and 1×10−4 M respectively. The non-stop evolution of nascent hydrogen of BH4 - kept the reaction mixing throughout the reduction tenure.

The antibacterial tests of the RGO-Ag composites were done by using method of inhibition zone. A common gram-negative bacterium, Escherichia coli (E. coli) found generally in lower intestine of warm-blooded organisms was chosen as model/indicator for antibacterial test.

The Gram-negative E. coli cells were cultured in sterile clean room. All the equipment for the E. coli cell culture was sterilized at high pressure and high temperature in an autoclave. The culture of E. coli was carried out in sterile Luria–Bertani (LB) media (containing 1.0g bacto-yeast extract, 2.0g bacto-tryptone and 1.0g NaCl in 200mL deionized water). A portion of the E. coli bacteria colony was taken from LB agar plates and incubated in LB media (5mL). The bacteria cell cultures were grown at 37°C in an incubator-shaker. After the overnight shaking, 1mL culture media was centrifuged at 10,000rpm for 10min to discard the supernatant and washed with PBS buffer and was suspended in 1mL of PBS buffer.

In order to accomplish inhibition zone test, filter paper was cut in disk shape and immerged in deionized water with sample concentration of 1mg/mL and dried. 200µL of the activated E. coli were homogenously spread over agar plates by a triangular spreader. Then the soaked filter paper disks were placed in the above mentioned plates followed by incubation at 37°Cfor 24h in biochemical incubators to check the zone of inhibition. The inhibition zones were measured by the areas out circled of the disk shaped filter paper without bacterial growth.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) were executed on X-PERTPRO PANalytical diffractometer using Cu Kα X-ray source (λ=1.5406) at a scanning rate of 1omin having generator voltage 40kV (stabilized current 30mA). UV–vis absorption spectra of the materials were monitored by Perkin-Elmer Lambda 35 spectrophotometer in deionized water. The average hydrodynamic diameter (AHD) was measured by Zetasizer Nano-ZS90 (Malvern Inc.). 1H NMR of gelatin C-dots was performed in a Bruker 600MHz instrument. The morphological studies were evaluated by using high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, JEOL-JEM-2100 with 200kV accelerating voltage). Energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) and mapping were done by field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, MERLIN, maximum acceleration voltage of 30kV). The surface chemical character was evaluated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Kratos Axis Ultra DLD) using Al-Kr X-ray source (1486.6eV). Thermo gravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried out by shimadzu, TA-50; Japan. Raman spectroscopy was performed in Trivista 555 spectrograph (Princeton Instruments) at 647nm excitation from Kr+ laser (Coherent, Sabre Innova SBRC-DBWK, power 10–12mW). Acquisition time of spectra collection was 120s for each specimen in the spectrum range of 1000–1800cm−1. Electrical characterizations were performed by an Ecopia HNS 5300 and potentiostat–galvanostat (Autolab) using 1M H2SO4 electrolyte. DC resistivity was evaluated by using Agilent 34401A 6½ digit multimeter (circular copper electrode of area=1.327 cm2) at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed mechanistic illustration for RGO-Ag synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: Herein, we applied a simple sonochemical green process to prepare reduced graphene oxide-silver (RGO-Ag) nanocomposite. Green synthesis was performed by using gelatin derived carbon dots (g-CDs) as a green reducing agent after sonochemical disassembling of AgNO3 in presence of GO. The formation of g-CDs was confirmed by FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. S1), photoluminescence spectroscopy, UV–vis spectroscopy (Figs. S2 and S3) and 1H NMR (Fig. S4). The TEM image of the g-CDs has been shown in Fig. S5. According to the FTIR and 1H NMR report and the cited literatures, it has already been known that g-CDs were formed by thermal coupling of gelatin polypeptides.[42] These products are very much prone to self-polymerisation, cyclisation, aromatization and condensation. Such behaviours can exothermally coalesce together which results nuclear burst at critical supersaturation concentration to yield C-dots.[43] The reduction mechanism of graphine oxide (GO) to reduced graphene oxide (RGO) still an open question. But the plausible reduction pathway can be hypothesized as SN2 nucleophilic reaction followed by ejection of water molecules as shown in Fig. 2 . The surface amine functionalities have a high chance to attack the epoxy carbon atoms to initiate the ring opening reaction. Such reaction generally estimated to be done in slightly alkaline environment. Our prepared g-CDs have pH of around 7.8 which is very much suitable for nucleophilic attack towards the epoxy carbon via its bare lone pair situated in nitrogenous groups. This back side SN2 attack forces to release water from the graphene oxide basal plane. Similar fashion also has been followed in case of hydroxyl functionalities. The reduction mechanism has been proposed by initial physisorption of silver ions on to GO surface. Later on ultrasonic irradiation affects to top down the GO cluster into microsized sheets (tactoids) by means of acoustic waves which results a mechanical agitation called cavitation. The speed of ultrasonic waves is around 1000–1500m/s and it can vary more or less 10cm down to 100mm over a wide frequency domain of 20kHz to 15MHz which is definitely much greater than the molecular dimensions [44,45]. Hence there is no such chemical encounter between the chemical species and ultrasonic waves, but instead of some physical phenomenon called acoustic cavitation. The graphical illustration of the ultrasonic wave activity has been demonstrated in Fig. 3 Having the high localized heat (approx. 5000K), high pressure (approx. 1000atm), immense heating-cooling rates (>109 K/s) and abrupt liquid jet streams (approx. 400km/h) generated by ultrasonication become hugely effective to disperse the GO in water homogeneously which results improved rate of diffusion of Ag+ onto the GO basal planes. For GO sheets, which are much larger than the acoustically derived bubbles during cavitation, may experience another phenomenon like microjets [46–49]. This also helped to dislodge the GO planes from there graphitic array. The surface functionalities of GO can interact with the incoming Ag+ through electrostatic attraction. This ionic attachment was also enhanced in presence of g-CDs due to high surface charge density due to their nanosized architecture. Therefore, RGO-Ag formed in a single step without consuming much time and other toxic solvents or reducing agents.

The XRD patterns were depicted in Fig. 4 a. From the figure, it has been noticed that GO exhibited a sharp peak at 11.2o for (002) crystal plane with an interlayer spacing of 0.82nm [50]. In case of RGO-Ag composite, the dominating peak almost diminished due to partial reduction of GO by ultrasonication and a new broad peak was come around 26.3o with an interlayer spacing of 0.44nm which corresponds to the (002) plane of reduced GO. This result can confirm the reduction of oxygenated functional groups to a lesser amount which collapses the interlayer spacing relative to GO [51]. The characteristic peaks of Ag were also prominent at the 2θ value of 38o (111), 44.2o (200), 64.6o (220) and 77.3o (311) which are due to the cubic crystal of silver (JCPDS File No. 04–0783). The results can suggest that the g-CDs can reduce GO and Ag+ via ultrasonic waves.

The UV–vis spectra of GO and RGO-Ag are shown in Fig. 4b. GO showed a typical absorption peak at 228nm for π-π∗ transition of aromatic C–C bond [52]. The peak of GO red shifted to 268nm because of the regeneration of structural array among the extensive conjugated sp2 hybridized carbon atoms. Meanwhile, a new absorption peak was observed at 429nm which is due to the formation of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs). The peak can be implied to the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) band of the grown AgNPs [53]. This result can support about the formation of AgNPs decorated RGO nanocomposite. The UV–visible spectra also gave some information about the nanoparticles’ shape and structural properties which is directly reflected by its particle size. In general, spherical, disk shaped and triangular/prism like nanosheets of silver normally shows one, two and/or multi-peaks respectively. Here the unique peak at 429nm confirmed that the AgNPs shape is purely spherical which are deposited on to RGO planes. Such results have also been confirmed after visualization through TEM images [18].

Morphological character was observed by TEM study which is documented in Fig. 5 (a-c). Fig. 5a shows the bare surface of GO with stacked morphology. After the sonochemical reduction the RGO-Ag shows Ag nanoparticles decorated randomly on RGO basal plane which is shown in Fig. 5b. Besides this, the agglomerated silver nanoparticles on the graphene surface prohibit the restacking of the nanosheets, resulting in improved electron transport property. Similar work also has been reported earlier [54]. Selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (Fig. 5b-inset) shows 4 concentric circles which are implied to characteristic lattice planes of ‘fcc’ Ag; (111), (200), (220) and (311). The prominent concentric circular array in SAED pattern confirms that the grown quasi-spherical Ag nanoparticles (22–40nm) on graphene surface are highly crystalline even at nanoscale. The image of lattice fringe was calculated to be 0.242nm which has been pictured in Fig. 5c. This value also persists with the XRD results of the composite.

Fig. 6 (a-c) is the FESEM images of GO, and silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) decorated RGO sheets. Fig. 6a shows the GO sheets prepared by ultrasonic treatment of GO in aqueous medium and Fig. 6b represents the RGO-Ag nanocomposites. The AgNPs having diameter in the range of 20–40nm were distributed on the RGO sheet surfaces. The Ag content in the prepared materials was investigated by EDX and the distribution was monitored by mapping. The EDX spectra in Fig. 6c showed C, O and Ag content in RGO-Ag composite. The amount of Ag was found to be 4.69 atom%. The mapping of Ag nanaoparticles in the selected area of the prepared composite was pictured also in Fig. 6c (inset). Therefore, according to EDX spectra it can be affirmed that RGO-Ag nanocomposites have been successfully synthesized by the sonochemical green method adopted here.

To investigate the surface chemical properties, material compositions and bonding, XPS technique is as a powerful characterizing tool. XPS of RGO-Ag was performed to monitor the quantitative analysis of the nanocomposite. The XPS spectrum in Fig. 7 (a) showed the characteristic peaks of Ag and C in the specimen which supports the formation of RGO-Ag. The 3d spectrum of Ag at 368.2eV and 374.6eV was also showed in Fig. 7(b). These both peaks of Ag imply the binding energy of Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2 of elemental silver respectively. The peaks at 284.5eV and 372.6eV the high resolution C1s spectrum in Fig. 7(c) represents the deconvoluted spectra of the RGO-Ag composite where a clear peak was observed at 285.2eV for CC single bond. The peaks for CO, CO and COOH were also exhibited in the aforementioned deconvoluted spectra of C1s [55].

Raman spectra of GO and RGO-Ag were given in Fig. S6. Basically Raman spectra categorize two very primary attributes: first one is the G vibration mode related to the first order phonons’ scattering of E2g by aromatic sp2 carbons of graphene sheets and second one is D band due to breathing mode vibration of j-point phonon with A1g symmetry [56]. For GO and RGO-Ag the G band peak was noticed 1575 and 1602cm−1 respectively and the D-band was situated 1336 and 1355cm−1 respectively. The D/G ratio (ID/IG) pertained to the structural attributes of the prepared materials. The structural asperities go higher with the higher ID/IG value means. The GO displayed ID/IG value of ∼1.01 which implies to the structural defects due to the oxygenated functionalities in surfaces. After ultrasound assisted bio-reduction, the ID/IG value becomes 0.92 for RGO-Ag which can support the partial removal of oxygenated surface functional groups. This result may propose lowering of structural faults which outcomes better electron conductivity than GO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and Zeta potential analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The average hydrodynamic diameter (AHD) of GO, and RGO-Ag nanocomposites was measured by DLS as shown in Fig. S7. Assuming GO a spherical shaped nanoparticle, AHD of GO was found to be ∼322nm whereas the prepared RGO-Ag by C-dots surprisingly reduced to ∼122nm. Zeta potentials of GO and RGO-Ag were also evaluated in aqueous environment to evaluate the surface charges and stability of the prepared nano-composites (Fig. S8). The average surface charge value of GO was quantified to be ∼−17.9mV. The high negative value corresponds to the surplus amount of negative charges present in the GO surface. The high negative charge implies better water tolerability and longtime stability. In case of RGO-Ag the zeta potential value showed more negative (−31.8mV) which can reveal better stability of RGO-Ag over GO. The high surface charge of RGO-Ag corresponds to electrostatic repulsion among the nanoparticles, as a result better colloidal stability may achieved.

Fig. S9 is the thermometric decay plot of pristine graphite, GO, and ultosonically prepared RGO-Ag composites. Pristine graphite had a minor change in its weight loss behaviour which is around 2 wt% in the experimental temperature profile (up to 800°C). The oxidized material by Hummer’s method showed relatively lower thermal stability than pristine graphite due to lack of van der Waals interactive forces between inter-layers. There was a drastic mass loss noticed in GO degradation curve near 150°C because of the thermal degradation of the as generated functional groups in GO basal planes. Chemical reduction can partially diminish the oxygenated functional groups on GO surfaces. It can imply better stability for RGO with respect to GO. For RGO-Ag nanocomposite, there was a nominal mass loss below 150°C.

The catalytic activity of RGO-Ag nanocomposite was confirmed through 4-NP reduction to 4-aminophenol (4-AP) in the presence of borohydride as a reductant.[57] It has been known from several literatures that 4-AP is very useful chemical for applications in analgesic, antipyretic, photographic developer, anti-corrosion additive etc [36]. Hence to minimize the effect of toxicity of 4-NP and for efficient production of 4-AP, noble metal nanoparticles become a common practice for researchers. Therefore, 4-NP reduction in presence of borohydride was used as a model system to quantify the catalytic properties of RGO-Ag nanocomposite. Though the 4-NP to 4-AP conversion is a thermodynamically feasible reaction involving E0 for 4-NP/4-AP=−0.76 V and H3BO3/BH4 - =- 1.33 V versus SHE, it is kinetically throttled in absence of catalyst. In the absence of the RGO-Ag the mixture showed a strong absorption peak at 400nm for 4-NP in alkaline medium [58]. Initially in alkaline environment the 4-NP generates 4-nitrophenolate anion (λ max =400nm) which was monitored by spectrophotometry (Fig. 8 a). Curve (i) in the graph showed the absorption spectrum for 4-NP solely without borohydride and catalysts. After addition of borohydride the peak shift and gradual lowering of the peak height imply the catalytic reduction of 4-NP to 4-AP. The isobestic points were found at approximately 322nm showed that 4-AP was the unique product of this reduction process [59]. It was also been observed that after addition of the RGO-Ag catalyst, the reduction started instantaneously, i.e. there was no induction period observed. Sometimes dissolved oxygen creates hindrance in reduction which was also reported by some researchers. Thus nitrogen purging was done before reduction process. But in this case there was no such actions taken. This might be more beneficial in industrial point of view. Same test was performed in absence of graphene oxide. Silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) are itself stabilized with g-CDs dispersion. But as shown in Fig. S10, the AgNPs took longer time (15min) than the RGO-Ag solid state catalyst. Thus it is evident from our test that nanosurface decorated with noble metal nanoparticles have more proficiency in catalytic activity towards 4-NP.

The effect of borohydride concentration was also taken for judgment in reduction behaviour and reaction rate estimation. As per Fig. 8b it was found that in the borohydride concentration range of 0.02–0.06M, the rate increased monotonously but beyond 0.06M the rate change almost indifferent. That is why we selected the borohydride concentration at 0.1M which is higher concentration than 4-NP. Normally borohydride is very much prone to decompose in aqueous medium before starting the reduction, thus slightly excess amount of borohydride was taken to overcome this lack of reductant concentration in medium. The kinetic plot for the estimation of order of the reaction, the discoloration of 4-NP was monitored spectrophotometrically with time (Fig. 8c). Varying the borohydride concentration, the plot of ln[A] (absorbance at λ max =400nm) versus time revealed the gradual increment of K (apparent rate constant) with increasing borohydride concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reduction kinetics of 4-NP by RGO-Ag nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 8d is the time-dependency of the absorbance values at 400nm for 4-NP reduction at ambient temperature (28°C) with variable 4-NP concentration (0.0274–0.112mM). Throughout the experiment NaBH4 to 4-NP concentration and catalyst dose (3mg/100mL) were kept constant. In our case it was noticed that the 4-NP reduction was committed within 8–22min, increasing with the initial feed (4-NP) concentration. The gradual decrement in 4-NP concentration with time was revealed in Fig. 8d. The corresponding data revealed that the reduction followed the pseudo-first order kinetics. The calculated pseudo-first order rate constant (K), goodness of fit and turnover frequencies were tabulated in Table 1 . After tallying our reduction process, we also came to know that this work is also comparable to the previously reported values. [60–62] Such results can support our sonochemistry induced green route to prepare an efficient catalyst. To a minute view to the Table 1, it can be observed that the pseudo-first order rate constant have a gradual decreasing trend with increasing feed concentration. Such trend can be implied to the diffusion controlled mechanism. Theoretically, electron transfer rate at the metal surface is normally affected by diffusion of 4-NP to metal surfaces, electron transfer through interfaces and the diffusion out of the product 4-AP from the surfaces. The rate can be conveyed as [63]:
(1)
1 /
K
obs
= ( 1 / 4 π
R
2
) [ ( 1 /
K
e
) + ( R / D ) ]
where R, D and K e correspond to the radius of AgNPs, diffusion co-efficient and electron transfer rate respectively. Another thing we have to mention that if the metal surface area is higher i.e. metal radius is very small, they possess very high reduction potential which results good feasibility of electron transfer from metal surfaces [63]. Hence it can be assumed that for heterogeneous charge transfer system, the electron transfer is dominated over diffusion phenomenon. This implies, K e tending to infinite, or, (1/K e) become zero. Therefore, the above equation can be reduced to Smoluchowski empirical formula:
(2)
1 /
K
obs
= ( 1 / 4 π
R
2
) [ ( R / D ) ]
Or,
K
obs
= 4 π RD
Eq. (2) implies that the rate of reduction was directly proportional to the diffusion co-efficient whereas, diffusion co-efficient was inversely related to the feed concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Turn of frequency (TOF) and reusability of the catalyst
ce:section.ce:para: There are various research works have been already focused on the catalytic reduction process of 4-NP [64]. For catalyst systems to estimate their efficiency, catalyst turnover number (TON) and turnover frequency (TOF) are the commonly uttered parameters. In case of heterogeneous catalysts, the TON is defined as a ratio of number of reactant molecules to per gram catalyst to convert feed to product [65]. TOF is calculates by normalized the TON with time. For RGO-Ag catalyst, the TOF was evaluated to be 9.8×1016 molecules/g/s where feed concentration (10−4 M) and catalyst dose (1.2g/L) were kept constant. For heterogeneous solid state supported catalysis some expensive nano dimensional high cost noble metals have been used which are needed to be reviving after use. Fig. S11 is the plot of relative activity vs number of reuse. It was noticed that the catalyst had no substantial deterioration in their activity after reuse for 5 cycles. This can corroborates that the RGO-Ag catalyst was not deactivated significantly during the reduction, washing and separation operations. A comparison table regarding the catalytic activities of various catalysts for the reduction of 4-NP is given in Table 2 . It is clearly noticed from Table 2 that the RGO-Ag nanocomposites surpassed former catalysts that are accounted in the literatures. Various polymer hydrogels were also reported with surface adsorbed AgNPs which were also effective catalyst for 4-NP reduction [66,67]. Au and Ag both types of nanoparticles have extensive studied in this context. But in case of this work our system exhibited very fast reduction with respect to the large fraction of contemporary works [36–40,68–72]. The reduction reaction was fulfilled in shorter period of time in the presence of the RGO-Ag catalyst. Besides this, the RGO-Ag catalyst was synthesized via a simple, green and eco-friendly method using aqueous C-dots dispersion derived from gelatin without utilizing any harmful reducing or surfactant template.

Antibacterial behaviour of the prepared nanocomposite was primarily determined by using measuring the zone of inhibition. Fig. 9 depicts the digital photograph of agar plate with dipped paper disk where inhibition zone of the RGO-Ag nano-composite against E. coli was seen. The presence of the evident zones in the test testifies that the RGO-Ag nano-composites could suppress the bacterial growth of E. coli effectively. When the concentration of the RGO-Ag nano-composite was 1g/L, the zone of inhibition for E. coli is 2.1cm.

The antibacterial activity of the RGO-Ag nanocomposite is considered as a widely-accepted ‘capturing and killing’ mechanism which is graphically illustrated in Fig. 10 . The bacterium was initially get adhered onto the RGO surface because of their large specific surface area. The colloidal dispersion of RGO-Ag nanocomposite helps for effective contact with the bacterial cell wall. The large AgNPs in RGO sheets primarily interacted with the bacterium cell wall which is called capturing phenomenon of bacterium. After that the AgNPs can release Ag+ ions resulting production of reactive oxygen species (ROS). ROS is an essential material to destroy bacterial respiratory system which leads the bacteria to death. In this composite (RGO-Ag), RGO sheets can act as a nano-particle support with stabilization effect. This may cause more approachability of the AgNPs to bacterial cell wall. Here AgNPs may penetrate through the cell wall membrane of bacteria and extending to the bacteria deactivation. Such activity of the nanocomposite can restrict the bacterial growth via capturing-killing mechanism in a certain region.

To further investigate the influence of RGO-Ag nanocomposite on E. coli cells, the morphological changes of bacterial cells without and with the treatment of RGO-Ag were visualized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). As depicted in Fig. 11 the untreated E. coli cells disclosed smooth cell walls and clear edges. After overnight incubation with RGO-Ag at 37°C, the bacterial cell surfaces became wrinkled and damaged, proposing that the antibacterial mechanism involved the disruption of bacterial cell walls.

The sonochemical green synthesis to prepare reduced graphene oxide decorated with silver nanoparticles was prepared successfully. The XRD, HRTEM and XPS supported the effectiveness of the sonochemical method which leads to the de-stacking of the GO and reduction of Ag+. The spherical shape of AgNPs adhered over GO sheets were confirms by UV–vis spectra and later on was visualized in HRTEM high magnified images. The XPS revealed the decrement of oxygenated functionalities present in GO which also another support of sonochemical reduction. The material performed a superior catalyst agains 4-Nitrophenol reduction within 7min having a reusuability of at least 5 times (not lowering the performance of 0.93 relative activity). The exposed surface area and low particle size of RGO-Ag nanomaterial have a profound catalytic effect. Besides this, the catalyst showed antibacterial effect via ‘attack and kill’ mechanism with drastic breakage of cell-membrane and it revealed an inhibition zone of 2.1cm against E. coli.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.ultsonch.2017.05.005.
